texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two  types of modules  are  most common in  gas  permeation: the  hollow fibre module  and the  spiral wound  module. With some  simplifying  assumptions regarding  the  flow pattern, the  separation characteristics of  such modules  can be calculated for  binary  and ternary  mixtures.
texts.text: Two  types of modules  are  most common in  gas  permeation: the  hollow fibre module  and the  spiral wound  module. With some  simplifying  assumptions regarding  the  flow pattern, the  separation characteristics of  such modules  can be calculated for  binary  and ternary  mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More important in  practice, however, is  the  separation of  multicomponent  mixtures.  This  paper  discusses the  design of multicomponent systems including  cases  where non-permeating components or  carrier gases  at  the  permeate side are  present.
texts.text: More important in  practice, however, is  the  separation of  multicomponent  mixtures.  This  paper  discusses the  design of multicomponent systems including  cases  where non-permeating components or  carrier gases  at  the  permeate side are  present.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results of  some  calculations are  discussed and compared  with the  usual  short-cut method  based  on the  assumption  of  a  pseudo-binary  mixture.  The results demonstrate  that  the  reduction  of  a  multicomponent mixture to  a  pseudobinary mixture is  only reasonable  when  components  of  similar permeability  are  lumped  together. Serious  deviations with  respect to  membrane area  or  product  composition must be expected  for  larger differences in  permeabilities.
texts.text: The results of  some  calculations are  discussed and compared  with the  usual  short-cut method  based  on the  assumption  of  a  pseudo-binary  mixture.  The results demonstrate  that  the  reduction  of  a  multicomponent mixture to  a  pseudobinary mixture is  only reasonable  when  components  of  similar permeability  are  lumped  together. Serious  deviations with  respect to  membrane area  or  product  composition must be expected  for  larger differences in  permeabilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fiir  die  Trennung  von Gas-Gemischen  durch Permeation sind  Hohlfasermodule und  Wickelmodule  entwickelt  worden. Unter einigen  vereinfachenden  Annahmen hinsichthch  der  Stromftihrung  der  Case in  den Modulen  und bei  Kenntnis der Permeabilitiiten der  Komponenten ist es  fur  bin&e  Gemische moglich,  die  Tremrcharakteristik dieser Module zu  berechnen und zu optimieren.
texts.text: Fiir  die  Trennung  von Gas-Gemischen  durch Permeation sind  Hohlfasermodule und  Wickelmodule  entwickelt  worden. Unter einigen  vereinfachenden  Annahmen hinsichthch  der  Stromftihrung  der  Case in  den Modulen  und bei  Kenntnis der Permeabilitiiten der  Komponenten ist es  fur  bin&e  Gemische moglich,  die  Tremrcharakteristik dieser Module zu  berechnen und zu optimieren.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In der  Praxis  sind  jedoch weniger  binare  Mischungen, sondern Mehrkomponentengemische zu zerlegen. Es  wird  em Rechenverfahren vorgestellt, dass  die  Behandlung  von Mehrkomponentensystemen-unter Einbeziehung von 'inerten' Komponenten sowie  permeatseitigen Spiilstromen-ermoglicht.
texts.text: In der  Praxis  sind  jedoch weniger  binare  Mischungen, sondern Mehrkomponentengemische zu zerlegen. Es  wird  em Rechenverfahren vorgestellt, dass  die  Behandlung  von Mehrkomponentensystemen-unter Einbeziehung von 'inerten' Komponenten sowie  permeatseitigen Spiilstromen-ermoglicht.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anhand von  Beispielen  wird gezeigt, dass  die  iibliche Methode,  Mehrkomponentensysteme auf  ein  pseudobiniires System  zu reduzieren, nur dann  vertretbar ist, wenn  sich  die  Permeabilitiiten der  jeweils zusammengefassten  Komponenten  nur unwesentlich unterscheiden.  Im  Falle  stark  unterschiedlicher Permeabilitaten  fiihrt hingegen eine  solche Vereinfachung zu sehr  grossen  Fehlern bei der  Bestimmung  der  erforderlichen Membranflache  bzw. der  Produktzusammensetzung.
texts.text: Anhand von  Beispielen  wird gezeigt, dass  die  iibliche Methode,  Mehrkomponentensysteme auf  ein  pseudobiniires System  zu reduzieren, nur dann  vertretbar ist, wenn  sich  die  Permeabilitiiten der  jeweils zusammengefassten  Komponenten  nur unwesentlich unterscheiden. texts.text: unhindered flow'  model is  most appropriate  for spiral wound modules, while  the  simplifying  assumption of complete mixing  on  both  sides  of the membrane is  only reasonable for  some  plate  and frame  systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Weller and  Steiner first solved the cases of complete mixing [l] and  of plug flow  in the feed compartment and  unhindered flow  in the permeate compartment [2] for  binary  mixtures.  In  the  following  decade several authors discussed gas separation problems based  on  one or more of the above  four models.  Relevant  papers are, for example,  those of Brubaker  and  Kammermeyer [3], Oishi et al. [4],  Walawender and  Stern  [S,  61,  Pan  and Habgood [7] and  Sirkar [8,9].  All these papers, however, are limited  to  binary  or ternary mixtures,  while real gas  mixtures which have  to  be  separated  often contain five  or even more  components.
texts.text: Weller and  Steiner first solved the cases of complete mixing [l] and  of plug flow  in the feed compartment and  unhindered flow  in the permeate compartment [2] for  binary  mixtures.  In  the  following  decade several authors discussed gas separation problems based  on  one or more of the above  four models.  Relevant  papers are, for example,  those of Brubaker  and  Kammermeyer [3], Oishi et al. [4],  Walawender and  Stern  [S,  61,  Pan  and Habgood [7] and  Sirkar [8,9].  All these papers, however, are limited  to  binary  or ternary mixtures,  while real gas  mixtures which have  to  be  separated  often contain five  or even more  components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, general equations  are derived  for the separation of an N-component mixture  and  for all  four models. The  results  of some calculations will be  compared with results  based  on  the assumption of a  pseudobinary  mixture. The reduction of  a  multicomponent mixture  to a pseudo-binary  mixture  was  first suggested in  gas  permeation  by  Hogsett and Mazur  [IO].
texts.text: In this paper, general equations  are derived  for the separation of an N-component mixture  and  for all  four models. The  results  of some calculations will be  compared with results  based  on  the assumption of a  pseudobinary  mixture. The reduction of  a  multicomponent mixture  to a pseudo-binary  mixture  was  first suggested in  gas  permeation  by  Hogsett and Mazur  [IO].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  calculations are based  on  the following  assumptions.
texts.text: The  calculations are based  on  the following  assumptions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) The  N-component feed mixture  may contain one non-permeating component.
texts.text: The  N-component feed mixture  may contain one non-permeating component.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) The  permeate consists of N  -  1 components in case 1  (complete mixing) and case  2 (plug  flow/ unhindered flow). In  cases 3 and  4 (co- and  countercurrent flow) the permeate may consist  of  N1 permeating components and  one  non-permeating component.  Furthermore, cases 3 and  4 allow the presence of a  carrier gas  at  the permeate  side.
texts.text: The  permeate consists of N  -  1 components in case 1  (complete mixing) and case  2 (plug  flow/ unhindered flow). In  cases 3 and  4 (co- and  countercurrent flow) the permeate may consist  of  N1 permeating components and  one  non-permeating component.  Furthermore, cases 3 and  4 allow the presence of a  carrier gas  at  the permeate  side.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) There  is  no  coupling  of fluxes,  i.e. the permeability of any  component in the mixture  is  identical  to the permeability of the pure component.
texts.text: There  is  no  coupling  of fluxes,  i.e. the permeability of any  component in the mixture  is  identical  to the permeability of the pure component.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (5) There  are negligible pressure Iosses  due to friction at  each side  of the membrane.
texts.text: (5) There  are negligible pressure Iosses  due to friction at  each side  of the membrane.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (6)  Backmixing due to  diffusive transport  can  be neglected in  the feed and permeate  compartments.
texts.text: Backmixing due to  diffusive transport  can  be neglected in  the feed and permeate  compartments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (7) There  is  negligible  concentration  polarization at each  side of the membrane (and in the porous  support layer  of  asymmetric membranes [ 11  I).
texts.text: (7) There  is  negligible  concentration  polarization at each  side of the membrane (and in the porous  support layer  of  asymmetric membranes [ 11  I).

texts.orig: Several methods are described  in the literature for solving  the necessary set  of equations in  the case  of two or three components: the method of Weller and Steiner [ 1,  21;  the  method of Huckins  and  Kammermeyer [ 11, 121;  and the iteration  method of Stern et al.  [I 31.
texts.text: Several methods are described  in the literature for solving  the necessary set  of equations in  the case  of two or three components: the method of Weller and Steiner [ 1,  21;  the  method of Huckins  and  Kammermeyer [ 11, 121;  and the iteration  method of Stern et al.  [I 31.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All methods are  sound; they differ only  in the sequence  of calculation.  In this  paper, a  variation  of  the iteration  method described in ref.  13 is  employed which is  advantageous  with  respect to  computing time. The  set of equations  which  can be solved by  Newton's  method is  listed in  Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most significant  fact in  model 2  is that the permeate flow  is regarded  as 'unhindered' locally,  i.e. the local  permeate fluxes of each component are completely determined by  the local  feed-side  concentration (and  local trans-membrane pressure  differences).  This assumption  is, for  example,  very common in  calculations for  reverse  osmosis modules employing asymmetric membranes.
texts.text: The most significant  fact in  model 2  is that the permeate flow  is regarded  as 'unhindered' locally,  i.e. the local  permeate fluxes of each component are completely determined by  the local  feed-side  concentration (and  local trans-membrane pressure  differences).  This assumption  is, for  example,  very common in  calculations for  reverse  osmosis modules employing asymmetric membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consequently, the  module permeate flow  and  its composition follow  from an integration of  the  local permeate  flows.
texts.text: Consequently, the  module permeate flow  and  its composition follow  from an integration of  the  local permeate  flows.

texts.orig: According to  Fig. 1, three  parallel  plug  flows  are the  essential feature  of  model 3.  Furthermore, the local permeate flux  is no longer  considered to  be 'unhindered'-the local  driving  force for  every permeating  component is influenced by the  respective local concentration partial  pressures in the  feed and  in the permeate.
texts.text: According to  Fig. 1, three  parallel  plug  flows  are the  essential feature  of  model 3.  Furthermore, the local permeate flux  is no longer  considered to  be 'unhindered'-the local  driving  force for  every permeating  component is influenced by the  respective local concentration partial  pressures in the  feed and  in the permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contrary to  models 1  and 2, the  cocurrent flow model allows  the  presence of  a  carrier gas  at  the permeate side. In practice, such  a  carrier  gas is convenient in  cases  where the  lowering of  the  partial pressures  at  the permeate  side  is  desired.
texts.text: Contrary to  models 1  and 2, the  cocurrent flow model allows  the  presence of  a  carrier gas  at  the permeate side. In practice, such  a  carrier  gas is convenient in  cases  where the  lowering of  the  partial pressures  at  the permeate  side  is  desired.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Naturally, the  balance  must be  formulated differentially.  The  set  of equations  which  has to be solved is listed in  Table  3. Essentially,  the  system contains  N first-order  differential  equations  which can  be  solved by  Runge-Kutta.
texts.text: Naturally, the  balance  must be  formulated differentially.  The  set  of equations  which  has to be solved is listed in  Table  3. Essentially,  the  system contains  N first-order  differential  equations  which can  be  solved by  Runge-Kutta.

texts.orig: Some results from numerical calculations will be discussed here as  an example.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this  context  the numerical  difficulties arising  from the cocurrent  and  countercurrent  flow models  must  be emphasized. A  set  of linear  equations  must  be solved in order  to obtain  the concentration  gradients at z/L  =  0. In  the  case  of  the  cocurrent  flow  model, a pivoted Gaussian  elimination  method has  to  be  employed. In the case of the countercurrent  flow model,  a  Newtonian search  algorithm has  to  be employed in  order  to minimize  the necessary number of iterations.
texts.text: In this  context  the numerical  difficulties arising  from the cocurrent  and  countercurrent  flow models  must  be emphasized. A  set  of linear  equations  must  be solved in order  to obtain  the concentration  gradients at z/L  =  0. In  the  case  of  the  cocurrent  flow  model, a pivoted Gaussian  elimination  method has  to  be  employed. In the case of the countercurrent  flow model,  a  Newtonian search  algorithm has  to  be employed in  order  to minimize  the necessary number of iterations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most important,  Figs. 2-8 emphasize the fact that the  influence  of  the  module flow  pattern  on  module selectivity and necessary membrane surface  area  is insignificant  for  membrane 'selectivities' >  2.5  (corresponding to separation factors oil  4 0.4).  Only  case 1, assuming complete mixing  on  both  sides of the membrane, must  always be considered  as  a 'worst  case'.
texts.text: Most important,  Figs. 2-8 emphasize the fact that the  influence  of  the  module flow  pattern  on  module selectivity and necessary membrane surface  area  is insignificant  for  membrane 'selectivities' >  2.5  (corresponding to separation factors oil  4 0.4).  Only  case 1, assuming complete mixing  on  both  sides of the membrane, must  always be considered  as  a 'worst  case'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should  be  noted that in any  case the maximum possible cut ratio,  indicated by  vertical  dashed  lines  in Fig.  2,  is  determined by the flow pattern in  the module: e  amax >  eZmav  7  earnax  >  I&,,. This  result would be lost if the  multicomponent system were  treated as a pseudo-binary  mixture!
texts.text: It should  be  noted that in any  case the maximum possible cut ratio,  indicated by  vertical  dashed  lines  in Fig.  2,  is  determined by the flow pattern in  the module: e  amax >  eZmav  7  earnax  >  I&,,. This  result would be lost if the  multicomponent system were  treated as a pseudo-binary  mixture!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of  interest  are the retentate concentrations  of component '2'  (Fig.  4). According to Fig. 4, the retentate concentration  x2 can be lower  than  the feed concentration (in this case  for cut  ratios 0 7  0.6), a mode of operation which  should be avoided.
texts.text: Of  interest  are the retentate concentrations  of component '2'  (Fig.  4). According to Fig. 4, the retentate concentration  x2 can be lower  than  the feed concentration (in this case  for cut  ratios 0 7  0.6), a mode of operation which  should be avoided.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure  8 shows the influence of the non-permeating component '5'  on  the  permeate number R and  on  the membrane surface area.  It  emphasizes that nonpermeating carrier  gases,  especially  at  higher concentrations, reduce  the  driving force  and  consequently can lead to markedly higher amounts of membrane surface area. The  permeate-side  sweep-gas  cut ratio  BS  = Pa/Fa was  varied  between 0 and  1,  leading  to an increase  of the overall  cut ratio  of 10%  for R = 1 and 5%  for R = 2.5.
texts.text: Figure  8 shows the influence of the non-permeating component '5'  on  the  permeate number R and  on  the membrane surface area.  It  emphasizes that nonpermeating carrier  gases,  especially  at  higher concentrations, reduce  the  driving force  and  consequently can lead to markedly higher amounts of membrane surface area. The  permeate-side  sweep-gas  cut ratio  BS  = Pa/Fa was  varied  between 0 and  1,  leading  to an increase  of the overall  cut ratio  of 10%  for R = 1 and 5%  for R = 2.5.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: According to  Fig. 9, the  simplification can  lead to serious errors with  respect to the necessary  membrane surface  area. It should be  especially noted that the membrane area calculated by  the short-cut method is always smaller than  the  exact  figure-the short-cut method will  always be on the 'unsafe  side'!
texts.text: According to  Fig. 9, the  simplification can  lead to serious errors with  respect to the necessary  membrane surface  area. It should be  especially noted that the membrane area calculated by  the short-cut method is always smaller than  the  exact  figure-the short-cut method will  always be on the 'unsafe  side'!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure  10 shows  the differences in module permeate concentration for both  calculation methods. Depending on which components are  lumped together,  serious differences between the calculated permeate and retentate concentrations must be  expected.  This is  of special  importance for components with a low  concentration  in  the feed-like  impurities.
texts.text: Figure  10 shows  the differences in module permeate concentration for both  calculation methods. Depending on which components are  lumped together,  serious differences between the calculated permeate and retentate concentrations must be  expected.  This is  of special  importance for components with a low  concentration  in  the feed-like  impurities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, it  should  be  noted  that  the assumption of  a  pseudo-binary mixture will lead  only  to  overall permeate and retentate  concentrations: since  several components are  lumped together  it is impossible  to predict the corresponding concentrations of the individual  components in  the feed and in  the permeate!
texts.text: Furthermore, it  should  be  noted  that  the assumption of  a  pseudo-binary mixture will lead  only  to  overall permeate and retentate  concentrations: since  several components are  lumped together  it is impossible  to predict the corresponding concentrations of the individual  components in  the feed and in  the permeate!
